narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a solid upper-middle-class family background, you've put significant effort into your law school journey, including choosing full-time studies and building a respectable undergraduate record. The admissions model we use evaluates applicants based on patterns from past students' paths to the bar exam, predicting whether you'd pass on your first try. For you specifically, it estimated a 45% chance of failing the bar exam, which crossed our threshold for denial to ensure we select those most likely to succeed right away. The biggest factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2nd, which is notably lower than the typical student's 5th or 6th decile and signaled a potential dip in later academic momentum. On a brighter note, your strong 9th decile in Year 1 outperformed the average and strongly pulled the prediction back toward passing, showing your early potential as a standout. Your undergraduate GPA of 3.00, slightly below the usual 3.21, also helped nudge things positively but wasn't enough to offset other concerns. Meanwhile, your LSAT score of 27, well under the dataset average of about 37, contributed toward the failure side by highlighting a key area where you fell short of what typically predicts bar success. Compared to most applicants, who balance stronger test scores with consistent deciles across years, your profile showed promise early on but a concerning slide later that the model weighed heavily. Your full-time commitment and family income level offered minor boosts toward passing, aligning with the majority of successful students. While this rejection stings, it's not the end—consider retaking the LSAT to boost that score closer to average, strengthening your later-year performance through targeted prep, or exploring part-time programs or alternative law paths that value your early strengths. Many students in similar spots have rebounded by addressing these gaps, and with your background, you're well-positioned to do the same. We're rooting for your next steps.
